---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
# bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl
---

# Biophysical foundation of the Logistic-Normal Multinomial {#sec-lnm-biophysics}

In this section, we develop a thorough biophysical derivation of the
logistic-normal distribution on gene compositions. The core argument is that the
logistic-normal arises as the natural steady-state distribution of a system of
interacting genes under standard stochastic gene expression theory, in contrast
to the Dirichlet, which arises under the assumption that genes are
transcriptionally independent.

For completeness, all algebraic steps are provided. This is with the intention
that the reader can follow the derivation step by step, and verify for
themselves that each step is correct.

## The starting point: mRNA as a filtered readout of promoter activity {#sec-lnm-bio-filter}

Before introducing multi-gene interactions, we must clarify the identity of the
central variable in our model: the effective Poisson rate $\lambda_g$ that
appeared in both the Dirichlet-Multinomial derivation (@sec-dirichlet-multinomial)
and the Poisson-Gamma mixture representation of the negative binomial. This
subsection develops a microscopic foundation for $\lambda_g$ and for the
ordinary differential equation (ODE) that governs it.

### The two-state promoter model {#sec-lnm-bio-two-state}

Consider a gene $g$ whose promoter switches between an inactive (OFF) state and
an active (ON) state. Let the transition rates be $k_g^+$ (OFF $\to$ ON) and
$k_g^-$ (ON $\to$ OFF). When the promoter is ON, mRNA is produced at rate
$r_g$; when OFF, no transcription occurs. All mRNA molecules degrade at rate
$\gamma_g$, independently of the promoter state. We encode the promoter state
at time $t$ with an indicator random variable,

$$
\mathbb{1}_g(t) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if the promoter of gene } g \text{ is ON at time } t, \\
0 & \text{if the promoter of gene } g \text{ is OFF at time } t.
\end{cases}
$${#eq-lnm-bio-indicator}

### The mRNA ODE for a fixed promoter trajectory {#sec-lnm-bio-mrna-ode}

Suppose we freeze a particular realization of the promoter switching process,
denoting it $\mathbb{1}_g^*(t)$. That is, from the ensemble of all possible
switching trajectories, we pick one specific sequence of ON and OFF intervals.
Conditioned on this fixed trajectory, the expected mRNA abundance $\langle m_g
\rangle$ evolves deterministically according to

$$
\frac{d\langle m_g \rangle_{\mathbb{1}^*}}{dt} =
r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(t) - \gamma_g \langle m_g \rangle_{\mathbb{1}^*}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-mrna-ode}

The first term is the instantaneous production (nonzero only when the promoter
is ON), and the second term is first-order mRNA degradation. This is a standard
linear ODE of the form $\dot{y} + \gamma_g y = r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(t)$, which
we now solve.

### Solving the mRNA ODE by the integrating factor method {#sec-lnm-bio-mrna-solve}

To solve @eq-lnm-bio-mrna-ode, we use the integrating factor method. Dropping
the subscript $\mathbb{1}^*$ for readability and writing $\langle m_g \rangle$
as $\bar{m}_g$, the ODE in standard form is

$$
\dot{\bar{m}}_g(t) + \gamma_g \bar{m}_g(t) = r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ode-standard}

We multiply both sides by the integrating factor $e^{\gamma_g t}$:

$$
e^{\gamma_g t} \left[
\dot{\bar{m}}_g(t) + \gamma_g \bar{m}_g(t)
\right] =
e^{\gamma_g t} \, r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ode-multiply}

The left-hand side is the derivative of the product
$\bar{m}_g(t) \, e^{\gamma_g t}$, since by the product rule,

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\bar{m}_g(t) \, e^{\gamma_g t}\right) =
\dot{\bar{m}}_g(t) \, e^{\gamma_g t} +
\gamma_g \bar{m}_g(t) \, e^{\gamma_g t}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-product-rule}

Therefore, we can write

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\bar{m}_g(t) \, e^{\gamma_g t}\right) =
e^{\gamma_g t} \, r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ode-product}

Integrating both sides from an initial time $t_0$ to $t$:

$$
\bar{m}_g(t) \, e^{\gamma_g t} - \bar{m}_g(t_0) \, e^{\gamma_g t_0} =
r_g \int_{t_0}^{t} ds \; e^{\gamma_g s} \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(s).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ode-integrated}

Solving for $\bar{m}_g(t)$ by multiplying both sides by $e^{-\gamma_g t}$:

$$
\bar{m}_g(t) =
\bar{m}_g(t_0) \, e^{-\gamma_g(t - t_0)} +
r_g \int_{t_0}^{t} ds \; e^{-\gamma_g(t - s)} \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(s).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ode-full-solution}

This is the full solution. The first term is the contribution from the initial
condition, which decays exponentially with time constant $1/\gamma_g$. The
second term is a convolution of the promoter state with an exponential kernel.

### The steady-state solution: mRNA as an exponential moving average {#sec-lnm-bio-ema}

If we let the initial time recede to the distant past, $t_0 \to -\infty$, the
transient term vanishes (since $e^{-\gamma_g(t - t_0)} \to 0$), and we obtain
the steady-state solution:

$$
\bar{m}_g(t) =
r_g \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; e^{-\gamma_g(t - s)} \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(s).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ss-solution}

Changing variables to $\tau = t - s$ (so that $d\tau = -ds$; when $s = t$ we
have $\tau = 0$, and when $s \to -\infty$ we have $\tau \to \infty$):

$$
\bar{m}_g(t) =
r_g \int_0^{\infty} d\tau \; e^{-\gamma_g \tau} \, \mathbb{1}_g^*(t - \tau).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ss-convolution}

This result has a transparent physical meaning: **the steady-state mRNA
abundance is a weighted average of the promoter's past activity, with
exponential weights of timescale $1/\gamma_g$.** Recent promoter states
contribute more to the current mRNA count than distant ones, because the mRNA
molecules produced during distant ON periods have since degraded. The mRNA
degradation rate $\gamma_g$ sets the memory window of this averaging.

### Defining the memory-weighted promoter activity $p_g(t)$ {#sec-lnm-bio-p-def}

We can separate the maximal production capacity $\hat{r}_g = r_g / \gamma_g$
(the mean mRNA count that would accumulate if the promoter were permanently ON)
from the fraction of that capacity that is realized, by defining

$$
p_g(t) \equiv
\gamma_g \int_0^{\infty} d\tau \; e^{-\gamma_g \tau} \,
\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-definition}

Here we have dropped the asterisk: $p_g(t)$ is now a **random variable**,
defined over the ensemble of all possible promoter switching trajectories. The
prefactor $\gamma_g$ ensures that $p_g(t) \in [0, 1]$, since the exponential
kernel integrates to 1:

$$
\gamma_g \int_0^{\infty} d\tau \; e^{-\gamma_g \tau} = 1.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-kernel-normalization}

With this definition, the steady-state mRNA abundance is simply

$$
\bar{m}_g(t) = \hat{r}_g \cdot p_g(t) = \frac{r_g}{\gamma_g} \cdot p_g(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-m-from-p}

The quantity $p_g(t)$ is the **memory-weighted fraction of time the promoter has
been ON**, where "fraction" is weighted by the mRNA survival kernel. It is *not*
the instantaneous promoter state (which is 0 or 1), nor the time-averaged
fraction of time spent ON over some arbitrary window. It is a specific, physically
motivated exponential moving average of the promoter's history, with the
averaging timescale set by the mRNA lifetime $1/\gamma_g$.

### The effective Poisson rate $\lambda_g$ {#sec-lnm-bio-lambda-def}

We now connect this to the Poisson rate that appeared in the Dirichlet-Multinomial
framework. In the Poisson-Gamma mixture representation of the negative binomial
(@sec-dirichlet-multinomial), the mRNA count for gene $g$ was written as

$$
m_g \mid \lambda_g \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_g),
\quad \lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, \beta).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-poisson-gamma-recap}

The effective Poisson rate $\lambda_g$ in this mixture is precisely the expected
mRNA abundance conditional on the regulatory state. From @eq-lnm-bio-m-from-p,
we identify

$$
\lambda_g(t) \equiv \hat{r}_g \cdot p_g(t) = \frac{r_g}{\gamma_g} \cdot p_g(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lambda-from-p}

This is the central variable of our model. Its physical interpretation is
unambiguous: **$\lambda_g$ is the expected mRNA abundance of gene $g$,
conditional on the current (memory-weighted) regulatory state.** It is neither a
"transcription rate" in the kinetic sense (which would have units of
molecules/time) nor the actual mRNA count (which is a discrete random variable).
It is the Poisson rate---the continuous-valued effective expression level that
determines the mean of the mRNA count distribution.

## Dynamics of the effective Poisson rate {#sec-lnm-bio-lambda-dynamics}

Having established what $\lambda_g$ represents, we now derive the ODE it
satisfies. This is the crucial step that grounds the GRN equation on firm
biophysical footing.

### Deriving the ODE for $p_g(t)$ {#sec-lnm-bio-p-ode}

We start by differentiating the definition of $p_g(t)$ from
@eq-lnm-bio-p-definition with respect to time:

$$
\frac{dp_g(t)}{dt} =
\frac{d}{dt}\left[
\gamma_g \int_0^{\infty} d\tau \; e^{-\gamma_g \tau} \,
\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau)
\right].
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-diff-start}

Since the integration limits do not depend on $t$, we can pass the derivative
through the integral (by the Leibniz rule):

$$
\frac{dp_g(t)}{dt} =
\gamma_g \int_0^{\infty} d\tau \; e^{-\gamma_g \tau} \,
\frac{d}{dt}\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-leibniz}

To evaluate the time derivative of $\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau)$, we introduce the
substitution $s = t - \tau$, so that $\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau) = \mathbb{1}_g(s)$.
By the chain rule,

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau) =
\frac{d\mathbb{1}_g(s)}{ds} \cdot \frac{ds}{dt}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-chain-rule}

Since $s = t - \tau$ and $\tau$ is the integration variable (independent of $t$),
we have $ds/dt = 1$. Therefore,

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\mathbb{1}_g(t - \tau) = \frac{d\mathbb{1}_g(s)}{ds}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-indicator-deriv}

Substituting back and changing the integration variable from $\tau$ to
$s = t - \tau$ (with $d\tau = -ds$, and limits $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow s = t$,
$\tau \to \infty \Rightarrow s \to -\infty$):

$$
\frac{dp_g(t)}{dt} =
\gamma_g \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \,
\frac{d\mathbb{1}_g(s)}{ds}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-diff-substituted}

### Integration by parts {#sec-lnm-bio-p-ibp}

We evaluate this integral using integration by parts. Let

$$
u = e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)}, \quad dv = \frac{d\mathbb{1}_g(s)}{ds} \, ds.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ibp-setup}

Then

$$
du = \gamma_g e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \, ds, \quad v = \mathbb{1}_g(s).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ibp-derivs}

Applying the integration by parts formula
$\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \, \frac{d\mathbb{1}_g(s)}{ds} =
\left[ e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \mathbb{1}_g(s) \right]_{-\infty}^{t} -
\gamma_g \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; \mathbb{1}_g(s) \, e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ibp-result}

Evaluating the boundary term at the limits:

- At $s = t$: $e^{-\gamma_g(t-t)} \mathbb{1}_g(t) = e^0 \cdot \mathbb{1}_g(t) = \mathbb{1}_g(t)$.
- At $s \to -\infty$: $e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \to 0$, so this term vanishes.

Therefore,

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \, \frac{d\mathbb{1}_g(s)}{ds} =
\mathbb{1}_g(t) -
\gamma_g \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; \mathbb{1}_g(s) \, e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ibp-evaluated}

### Recognizing $p_g(t)$ in the result {#sec-lnm-bio-p-recognize}

Substituting @eq-lnm-bio-ibp-evaluated back into @eq-lnm-bio-p-diff-substituted:

$$
\frac{dp_g(t)}{dt} =
\gamma_g \mathbb{1}_g(t) -
\gamma_g^2 \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; \mathbb{1}_g(s) \, e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-diff-intermediate}

Now, recall from the definition @eq-lnm-bio-p-definition that, after the
same change of variables $s = t - \tau$,

$$
p_g(t) = \gamma_g \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} \, \mathbb{1}_g(s).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-in-s}

Comparing with the second term in @eq-lnm-bio-p-diff-intermediate, we see that

$$
\gamma_g^2 \int_{-\infty}^{t} ds \; \mathbb{1}_g(s) \, e^{-\gamma_g(t-s)} =
\gamma_g \cdot p_g(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-recognize-p}

Therefore, the ODE for $p_g(t)$ simplifies to

$$
\boxed{
\frac{dp_g(t)}{dt} = \gamma_g \left[ \mathbb{1}_g(t) - p_g(t) \right].
}
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-ode-result}

This is a key result. Between promoter jumps (i.e., when the indicator
$\mathbb{1}_g(t)$ is constant), the variable $p_g$ relaxes exponentially toward
the current promoter state:

- If the promoter is ON ($\mathbb{1}_g = 1$): $\dot{p}_g = \gamma_g(1 - p_g) > 0$,
  so $p_g$ increases toward 1.
- If the promoter is OFF ($\mathbb{1}_g = 0$): $\dot{p}_g = -\gamma_g p_g < 0$,
  so $p_g$ decreases toward 0.

The rate of this relaxation is $\gamma_g$---the mRNA degradation rate. This is
not a coincidence: it reflects the fact that the mRNA pool's "memory" of past
promoter activity decays at the rate at which mRNA molecules are destroyed.

### The ODE for $\lambda_g$ {#sec-lnm-bio-lambda-ode}

Using the relation $\lambda_g = \hat{r}_g \, p_g = (r_g / \gamma_g) \, p_g$
from @eq-lnm-bio-lambda-from-p, we can convert the ODE for $p_g$ into an ODE
for $\lambda_g$. Taking the time derivative:

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
\frac{r_g}{\gamma_g} \frac{dp_g}{dt}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lambda-diff-start}

Substituting @eq-lnm-bio-p-ode-result:

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
\frac{r_g}{\gamma_g} \cdot \gamma_g
\left[ \mathbb{1}_g(t) - p_g(t) \right] =
r_g \left[ \mathbb{1}_g(t) - p_g(t) \right].
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lambda-diff-substitute}

Now we express $p_g$ in terms of $\lambda_g$: since $p_g = \lambda_g / \hat{r}_g
= \gamma_g \lambda_g / r_g$, we have

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g(t) - r_g \cdot \frac{\gamma_g \lambda_g}{r_g} =
r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g(t) - \gamma_g \lambda_g.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lambda-ode-single}

This is the ODE for the effective Poisson rate of a single gene with a two-state
promoter:

$$
\boxed{
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} = r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g(t) - \gamma_g \lambda_g.
}
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lambda-ode-boxed}

Every term in this equation has a clear physical meaning:

- **$r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g(t)$** is the instantaneous mRNA production rate:
  $r_g$ molecules per unit time when ON, zero when OFF.
- **$\gamma_g \lambda_g$** is the mRNA degradation term. It arises because
  the mRNA pool's memory of past promoter activity decays at rate $\gamma_g$.
  This is not "degradation of a rate"---it is the consequence of mRNA molecules
  having a finite lifetime. As old mRNA degrades, the effective Poisson rate
  $\lambda_g$ relaxes toward whatever the current production state dictates.
- **$\gamma_g$** is unambiguously the mRNA degradation rate---the same
  $\gamma$ that appears in every birth-death model of gene expression.

### Physical interpretation: the $\gamma_g \lambda_g$ term {#sec-lnm-bio-gamma-interpretation}

It is worth pausing on this point, because it resolves a potential confusion
that arises when the ODE for $\lambda_g$ is written down without the
microscopic derivation.

If one naively interprets $\lambda_g$ as a "transcription rate"---a kinetic
parameter with units of molecules per time---then the term
$\gamma_g \lambda_g$ appears to represent "degradation of a rate," which is
dimensionally valid but physically unclear. What degrades? The rate itself
doesn't have a physical substrate that can be destroyed.

The derivation above shows that this interpretation is unnecessary. The
variable $\lambda_g$ is not the transcription rate; it is the **expected mRNA
abundance**, which equals the Poisson rate in the intrinsic noise model. The
term $\gamma_g \lambda_g$ is literally the rate of mRNA destruction:
$\gamma_g$ molecules per unit time per molecule, times $\lambda_g$ molecules.
This is the standard first-order degradation kinetics that appears in every
model of mRNA turnover.

The mRNA degradation rate $\gamma_g$ simultaneously plays a dual role:

1. **In the birth-death process**: $\gamma_g$ is the rate constant for
   first-order mRNA degradation.
2. **In the memory filter**: $\gamma_g$ sets the timescale $1/\gamma_g$ of
   the exponential window over which the promoter's switching history is
   averaged. Fast-degrading mRNAs (large $\gamma_g$) have short memories and
   track the promoter closely; slow-degrading mRNAs (small $\gamma_g$) have
   long memories and average over many switching events.

These are two facets of the same physics, not a coincidence.

## From independent genes to gene regulatory networks {#sec-lnm-bio-grn}

### The independent-gene case: recovering the Dirichlet-Multinomial {#sec-lnm-bio-independent}

For non-interacting genes, each promoter switches autonomously: the indicator
$\mathbb{1}_g(t)$ for gene $g$ does not depend on the expression of any other
gene $g'$. In this case, each $\lambda_g$ evolves according to its own
independent ODE (@eq-lnm-bio-lambda-ode-boxed). The steady-state distribution
of $p_g$ for gene $g$ is determined by the interplay between the promoter
switching rates ($k_g^+, k_g^-$) and the mRNA degradation rate ($\gamma_g$).
As shown in the Beta-Poisson compound derivation (@sec-beta-origin), the
stationary distribution of $p_g$ is

$$
p_g \sim \text{Beta}(\hat{k}_g^+, \hat{k}_g^-),
$${#eq-lnm-bio-p-beta}

where $\hat{k}_g^+ = k_g^+ / \gamma_g$ and $\hat{k}_g^- = k_g^- / \gamma_g$
are the promoter switching rates non-dimensionalized by the mRNA degradation
rate. Since $\lambda_g = \hat{r}_g \cdot p_g$, a scaled Beta is equivalent to
a Gamma distribution (up to the standard bursty-promoter parameterization; see
@sec-bnb-nb-routes), and we recover

$$
\lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, \beta), \quad g = 1, \ldots, G,
$${#eq-lnm-bio-gamma-recover}

with the $\lambda_g$ independent across genes. Normalizing to a composition
yields the Dirichlet distribution, exactly as in the Dirichlet-Multinomial
derivation (@sec-dirichlet-multinomial):

$$
\rho_g = \frac{\lambda_g}{\sum_{j=1}^G \lambda_j},
\quad \underline{\rho} \sim \text{Dir}(\underline{r}).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-dirichlet-recover}

The critical feature of this result is that the composition $\underline{\rho}$
has a covariance structure fully determined by the concentration vector
$\underline{r}$, with no free parameters for cross-gene correlations. This is
the correct consequence of the assumption that each promoter operates
autonomously.

### Gene regulatory networks: genes are not isolated {#sec-lnm-bio-grn-coupling}

In reality, genes do not transcribe in isolation. They form **gene regulatory
networks** (GRNs) in which the product of one gene (typically a transcription
factor, or TF) modulates the transcription rate of another. Activation,
repression, and more complex regulatory motifs (feed-forward loops, toggle
switches, cascades) create coupling between the expression dynamics of different
genes. This coupling is the physical origin of the cross-gene correlations that
the Dirichlet model cannot represent.

To see how coupling enters the ODE for $\lambda_g$, consider what changes when
gene $g$'s promoter is no longer autonomous. In the independent model, the
promoter state $\mathbb{1}_g(t)$ switches with fixed rates $k_g^+$ and $k_g^-$.
In a GRN, the ON-rate $k_g^+$ depends on the concentrations of the
transcription factors that bind to gene $g$'s regulatory regions. These TF
concentrations are themselves gene products---they are proportional to the
expression levels $\lambda_{g'}$ of the TF-encoding genes $g'$.

We therefore write the effective regulatory input to gene $g$ as a function of
the entire expression vector:

$$
\mathbb{1}_g(t) \longrightarrow h_g(\underline{\lambda}(t)),
$${#eq-lnm-bio-coarse-grain}

where $h_g : \mathbb{R}_+^G \to [0, 1]$ is a continuous function that encodes
the regulatory logic of the network for gene $g$. The transition from the
discrete indicator $\mathbb{1}_g$ to the continuous function $h_g$ is a
coarse-graining step: we average over the fast promoter switching dynamics and
retain only their dependence on the slowly varying expression levels of the
regulators. This coarse-graining is valid when the promoter switching rates are
fast relative to the timescale on which the regulatory inputs change---a
standard assumption in systems biology.

Substituting this into @eq-lnm-bio-lambda-ode-boxed, we obtain the coupled ODE
for the effective Poisson rate of gene $g$ in a GRN:

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
\underbrace{r_g \, h_g(\underline{\lambda})}_{b_g(\underline{\lambda})}
- \gamma_g \lambda_g,
\quad g = 1, \ldots, G.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode}

This is a system of $G$ coupled ODEs. The function
$b_g(\underline{\lambda}) \equiv r_g \, h_g(\underline{\lambda})$ is the
effective production rate of gene $g$, which depends on the expression levels
of all other genes through the GRN. The degradation term
$\gamma_g \lambda_g$ is the same mRNA degradation that appeared in the
single-gene case---it does not change upon introducing gene-gene interactions.

### Explicit assumptions {#sec-lnm-bio-assumptions}

The derivation of @eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode rests on several assumptions, which we
now enumerate explicitly:

1. **mRNA equilibrium (fast timescale).** Given the current regulatory state
   (i.e., the current values of all $\lambda_{g'}$), the mRNA count of gene $g$
   has equilibrated to its conditional steady-state distribution:
   $m_g \mid \lambda_g \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_g)$ (or NB for bursty
   transcription). This requires the mRNA turnover timescale $1/\gamma_g$ to be
   short relative to the timescale on which the regulatory inputs change.

2. **Protein tracks mRNA (proportionality assumption).** The regulatory effect
   of gene $g'$ on gene $g$ is mediated through protein, but protein abundance
   is proportional to mRNA abundance:
   $\text{[protein]}_{g'} \propto \lambda_{g'}$. This allows writing the
   regulatory input as $h_g(\underline{\lambda})$ rather than introducing a
   separate protein layer. This assumption is valid when translation and protein
   turnover are fast (or at least constant) relative to the regulatory dynamics,
   or equivalently, when the protein-to-mRNA ratio is approximately constant
   across cells.

3. **Promoter coarse-graining.** The fast promoter switching dynamics are
   averaged out, replacing the discrete indicator $\mathbb{1}_g$ with the
   continuous regulatory function $h_g(\underline{\lambda})$. This requires
   promoter switching rates ($k_g^+, k_g^-$) to be fast relative to the rate
   at which the regulatory inputs $\lambda_{g'}$ change.

4. **First-order mRNA degradation.** mRNA molecules are degraded at a
   constant rate $\gamma_g$, independent of abundance and of the promoter
   state. This is standard and well-supported experimentally for most genes.

These assumptions are common in the gene expression modeling literature. When
they hold, the effective Poisson rate $\lambda_g$ evolves according to
@eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode, and the $\gamma_g$ in the degradation term is
unambiguously the mRNA degradation rate.

## Stochastic dynamics: the Chemical Langevin Equation {#sec-lnm-bio-cle}

In a single cell, the dynamics of @eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode are stochastic: mRNA
molecules are produced in discrete bursts and degraded stochastically. For
sufficiently high molecular counts---the regime relevant to most genes detected
in scRNA-seq---the Chemical Langevin Equation (CLE) provides a continuous-time
stochastic approximation to the discrete Chemical Master Equation:

$$
d\lambda_g =
\left[b_g(\underline{\lambda}) - \gamma_g \lambda_g\right] dt
+ \sigma_g(\underline{\lambda})\, dW_g(t),
\quad g = 1, \ldots, G,
$${#eq-lnm-bio-cle}

where $dW_g(t)$ are independent Wiener increments and
$\sigma_g(\underline{\lambda})$ is the state-dependent noise amplitude. For
bursty transcription, $\sigma_g^2(\underline{\lambda}) \propto \lambda_g$
(Poisson-like scaling at the burst level), possibly with burst-size corrections.

The CLE replaces the discrete jump process (production of individual mRNA
molecules) with a continuous diffusion. This approximation is valid when the
molecular counts are not too small, so that the discrete jumps can be
approximated by a continuous stochastic process. For lowly expressed genes with
only a few molecules, the CLE breaks down and the discrete Chemical Master
Equation (or its Gamma steady-state, i.e., the DM-side model) is more
appropriate.

## Working in log-abundance space {#sec-lnm-bio-log-space}

The logistic-normal places a Gaussian distribution on **log-ratios** of
abundances, not on abundances themselves. It is therefore natural to transform
the CLE to log-abundance space. Define

$$
x_g = \log \lambda_g, \quad g = 1, \ldots, G.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-log-def}

### Itô's lemma for the transformation {#sec-lnm-bio-ito-lemma}

Applying Itô's lemma to @eq-lnm-bio-cle yields the dynamics of $x_g$. Recall
that for a function $f(\lambda)$ of a diffusion process
$d\lambda = \mu \, dt + \sigma \, dW$, Itô's lemma states

$$
df = \left[ \mu f'(\lambda) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 f''(\lambda) \right] dt
+ \sigma f'(\lambda) \, dW.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ito-lemma-general}

For $f(\lambda) = \log \lambda$, we have $f'(\lambda) = 1/\lambda$ and
$f''(\lambda) = -1/\lambda^2$. Substituting into Itô's lemma with
$\mu = b_g(\underline{\lambda}) - \gamma_g \lambda_g$ and
$\sigma = \sigma_g(\underline{\lambda})$:

$$
dx_g =
\left[
\frac{b_g(\underline{\lambda}) - \gamma_g \lambda_g}{\lambda_g}
+ \frac{1}{2} \sigma_g^2(\underline{\lambda}) \cdot
\left(-\frac{1}{\lambda_g^2}\right)
\right] dt
+ \frac{\sigma_g(\underline{\lambda})}{\lambda_g}\, dW_g(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ito-expand}

Simplifying the drift:

$$
\frac{b_g(\underline{\lambda}) - \gamma_g \lambda_g}{\lambda_g} =
\frac{b_g(\underline{\lambda})}{\lambda_g} - \gamma_g.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-drift-simplify}

Combining, we obtain

$$
dx_g =
\left[
\frac{b_g(\underline{\lambda})}{\lambda_g}
- \gamma_g
- \frac{\sigma_g^2(\underline{\lambda})}{2\lambda_g^2}
\right] dt
+ \frac{\sigma_g(\underline{\lambda})}{\lambda_g}\, dW_g(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ito}

The Itô correction term $-\sigma_g^2 / (2\lambda_g^2)$ is a standard
consequence of the nonlinear transformation from linear to log scale. Two
properties of @eq-lnm-bio-ito are important:

1. **The drift depends on $\underline{\lambda}$** (i.e., on $\underline{x}$
   through $\lambda_g = e^{x_g}$). This is where the GRN coupling enters.
2. **The noise amplitude in log-space** is $\sigma_g / \lambda_g$. For
   Poisson-like scaling $\sigma_g \propto \sqrt{\lambda_g}$, this becomes
   $1 / \sqrt{\lambda_g}$: the noise in log-space is state-dependent but
   *decreasing* in abundance. For moderately to highly expressed genes,
   $\lambda_g$ fluctuates around its steady-state mean $\bar{\lambda}_g$,
   and the noise amplitude can be approximated as the constant
   $1 / \sqrt{\bar{\lambda}_g}$. This is the key approximation that yields
   additive noise in log-space.

## The Linear Noise Approximation in log-space {#sec-lnm-bio-lna}

Suppose the system has a stable steady state
$\underline{\bar{x}} = (\log\bar{\lambda}_1, \ldots, \log\bar{\lambda}_G)$,
defined by setting the deterministic drift to zero in @eq-lnm-bio-ito. The
**Linear Noise Approximation** (LNA) linearizes the dynamics around this
steady state. We expand the drift to first order in
$\delta\underline{x} = \underline{x} - \underline{\bar{x}}$:

$$
\text{drift}_g \approx
\sum_{g'=1}^{G} J_{gg'}\, \delta x_{g'},
$${#eq-lnm-bio-drift-linear}

where $J_{gg'} = \partial(\text{drift}_g) / \partial x_{g'}$ evaluated at
$\underline{\bar{x}}$ is the $(g,g')$ entry of the **Jacobian matrix** of the
GRN dynamics in log-space. The Jacobian encodes the biophysics of the network
directly: $J_{gg'} > 0$ means gene $g'$ activates gene $g$ (increasing $g'$
increases the drift of $g$), and $J_{gg'} < 0$ means repression. The diagonal
entries $J_{gg}$ encode each gene's intrinsic restoring force (degradation plus
autoregulation).

### The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process {#sec-lnm-bio-ou}

Combining the linearized drift with the constant-noise approximation, the SDE
becomes a multivariate **Ornstein--Uhlenbeck** (OU) process:

$$
d\underline{x} =
\underline{\underline{J}}\,
(\underline{x} - \underline{\bar{x}})\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, d\underline{W}(t),
$${#eq-lnm-bio-ou}

where $\underline{\underline{B}} = \text{diag}(1/\sqrt{\bar{\lambda}_1},
\ldots, 1/\sqrt{\bar{\lambda}_G})$ is the (now constant) noise matrix in
log-space.

Two approximations have been made to arrive at @eq-lnm-bio-ou, and their
validity should be noted:

1. **Linearization of the drift** around the deterministic steady state. This
   is valid when the fluctuations $\delta\underline{x}$ are small relative to
   the scale on which the nonlinear regulatory functions change
   appreciably---a standard and well-justified assumption for most genes in
   single-cell data, where the coefficient of variation in log-space is
   typically modest.
2. **Freezing the noise amplitude** at its steady-state value. This is valid
   when $\lambda_g$ does not fluctuate too far from
   $\bar{\lambda}_g$---the same regime where the drift linearization holds.
   For lowly expressed genes with very few molecules, both approximations
   degrade, and the exact Gamma steady-state of the birth-death process (i.e.,
   the DM-side model) may be more appropriate.

## Steady-state distribution: the Lyapunov equation {#sec-lnm-bio-lyapunov}

A fundamental result in the theory of stochastic processes is that the
steady-state distribution of a multivariate OU process with stable drift matrix
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ (all eigenvalues with negative real part,
guaranteeing stability) and constant diffusion is a **multivariate Gaussian**:

$$
\underline{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\bar{x}},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-mvn}

### Deriving the Lyapunov equation {#sec-lnm-bio-lyapunov-deriv}

To see why the covariance satisfies the Lyapunov equation, we start from the
OU process @eq-lnm-bio-ou. At steady state, the distribution of $\underline{x}$
is stationary, meaning that the covariance
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \langle \delta\underline{x} \,
\delta\underline{x}^\top \rangle$ does not change in time. Let us write the
deviation from the mean as
$\delta\underline{x}(t) = \underline{x}(t) - \underline{\bar{x}}$. From
@eq-lnm-bio-ou, this deviation satisfies

$$
d(\delta\underline{x}) =
\underline{\underline{J}}\, \delta\underline{x}\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, d\underline{W}(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-deviation-sde}

Over a small time interval $dt$, the change in $\delta\underline{x}$ is

$$
\delta\underline{x}(t + dt) =
\delta\underline{x}(t) +
\underline{\underline{J}}\, \delta\underline{x}(t)\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, d\underline{W}(t).
$${#eq-lnm-bio-discrete-step}

To compute how the covariance changes, we form the outer product
$\delta\underline{x}(t+dt)\, \delta\underline{x}(t+dt)^\top$ and take
the expectation, keeping terms up to first order in $dt$. Using
@eq-lnm-bio-discrete-step:

$$
\delta\underline{x}(t+dt)\, \delta\underline{x}(t+dt)^\top =
\left[\delta\underline{x} +
\underline{\underline{J}}\, \delta\underline{x}\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, d\underline{W}\right]
\left[\delta\underline{x} +
\underline{\underline{J}}\, \delta\underline{x}\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, d\underline{W}\right]^\top,
$${#eq-lnm-bio-outer-product}

where we suppress the time argument for brevity. Expanding the product and
retaining only terms up to order $dt$ (using the Itô rule
$d\underline{W}\, d\underline{W}^\top = \underline{\underline{I}}\, dt$
and discarding terms of order $(dt)^2$ and $dt \cdot d\underline{W}$):

$$
\delta\underline{x}(t+dt)\, \delta\underline{x}(t+dt)^\top \approx
\delta\underline{x}\, \delta\underline{x}^\top
+ \underline{\underline{J}}\, \delta\underline{x}\,
\delta\underline{x}^\top \, dt
+ \delta\underline{x}\, \delta\underline{x}^\top \,
\underline{\underline{J}}^\top \, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, \underline{\underline{B}}^\top \, dt.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-outer-expanded}

Taking the expectation of both sides and using
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}(t) = \langle \delta\underline{x}(t)\,
\delta\underline{x}(t)^\top \rangle$:

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}(t + dt) =
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}(t)
+ \underline{\underline{J}}\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}(t)\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}(t)\, \underline{\underline{J}}^\top\, dt
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, \underline{\underline{B}}^\top\, dt.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-cov-evolution}

Rearranging and dividing by $dt$:

$$
\frac{d\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}}{dt} =
\underline{\underline{J}}\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}
+ \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\, \underline{\underline{J}}^\top
+ \underline{\underline{B}}\, \underline{\underline{B}}^\top.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-cov-ode}

At **steady state**, the covariance is time-invariant, so
$d\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} / dt = 0$. Defining the diffusion matrix
$\underline{\underline{D}} = \frac{1}{2}
\underline{\underline{B}}\, \underline{\underline{B}}^\top$ and rearranging,
we obtain the **continuous Lyapunov equation**:

$$
\underline{\underline{J}}\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}
+ \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\, \underline{\underline{J}}^\top
+ 2\underline{\underline{D}} = 0.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lyapunov}

This equation states that the steady-state covariance is at a balance between
two forces: the drift $\underline{\underline{J}}$ contracts the distribution
toward the steady state (the terms involving $\underline{\underline{J}}$),
while the noise $\underline{\underline{D}}$ broadens it.

### The integral solution {#sec-lnm-bio-sigma-integral}

The Lyapunov equation @eq-lnm-bio-lyapunov can be solved explicitly. When
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ is stable (all eigenvalues with negative real
part), the solution is

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\int_0^\infty
e^{\underline{\underline{J}} t}\,
(2\underline{\underline{D}})\,
e^{\underline{\underline{J}}^\top t}\, dt.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-sigma-integral}

To verify this, one can differentiate the right-hand side with respect to the
upper limit and check that it satisfies the original covariance ODE
@eq-lnm-bio-cov-ode with the boundary condition
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}(0) = 0$ at $t = 0$ and convergence as
$t \to \infty$ (guaranteed by stability of $\underline{\underline{J}}$).

This integral solution shows that $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ is a
nonlinear but deterministic function of the network topology
($\underline{\underline{J}}$) and the noise structure
($\underline{\underline{D}}$). Crucially, **the off-diagonal entries of
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ are nonzero whenever
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ has off-diagonal entries**, i.e., whenever genes
regulate each other.

## From multivariate normal log-abundances to the logistic-normal {#sec-lnm-bio-to-ln}

We have arrived at @eq-lnm-bio-mvn: the steady-state distribution of
log-abundances is multivariate Gaussian. The passage to gene compositions
now follows by definition.

In single-cell RNA-seq, the observed count vector $\underline{u}^{(c)}$ for a
cell $c$ is a sample from a capture process applied to the cell's mRNA pool.
The composition vector $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$ represents the relative
abundance of each gene's mRNA:

$$
\rho_g^{(c)} = \frac{\lambda_g^{(c)}}{\sum_{j=1}^G \lambda_j^{(c)}}
= \frac{e^{x_g^{(c)}}}{\sum_{j=1}^G e^{x_j^{(c)}}}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-composition}

This is exactly the softmax (inverse ALR) transformation applied to the
log-abundances $\underline{x}^{(c)}$. Since $\underline{x}^{(c)}$ is Gaussian
by @eq-lnm-bio-mvn, $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$ follows a **logistic-normal
distribution** by definition (cf.\ @sec-lnm-logistic-normal-def):

$$
\underline{\rho}^{(c)} \sim
\text{LogisticNormal}(\underline{\mu}_{\text{ALR}},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{ALR}}),
$${#eq-lnm-bio-logistic-normal}

where $\underline{\mu}_{\text{ALR}}$ and
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{ALR}}$ are the mean and covariance of
the ALR-transformed log-abundances (a linear transformation of
$\underline{\bar{x}}$ and $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$; see
@sec-lnm-alr-to-clr for the ALR-to-CLR algebra).

This is the central result of this chapter: **the logistic-normal on gene
compositions is the steady-state distribution of a gene regulatory network
under the linear noise approximation in log-abundance space.** The covariance
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ is not an arbitrary statistical object---it is
the Lyapunov solution to the OU process governing the GRN, and its entries are
direct consequences of the regulatory interactions encoded in
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ and the bursty-transcription noise encoded in
$\underline{\underline{D}}$.

It is worth being explicit about where the biophysics ends and the
parameterisation begins. The LNA produces an unconstrained $G$-dimensional
Gaussian on log-abundances $\underline{x}^{(c)} \in \mathbb{R}^G$; the
compositional distribution follows by applying the softmax map
$\underline{\rho}^{(c)} = \text{softmax}(\underline{x}^{(c)})$, which
collapses the $G$-dimensional space onto the $(G-1)$-dimensional simplex.
The further step from "Gaussian on $\mathbb{R}^G$" to "ALR parameters
$\underline{\mu}_{\text{ALR}},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{ALR}}$" is **not** a step in the
biophysics: it is a downstream gauge-fixing convention that removes the one
redundant degree of freedom inherent to compositional data.

## The low-rank covariance as regulatory architecture {#sec-lnm-bio-low-rank}

The low-rank factorization
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{d})$ from
@eq-lnm-low-rank-cov acquires a natural interpretation in this biophysical
framework.

**The rank-$k$ factor $\underline{\underline{W}}$.** Gene regulatory networks
are sparse and modular: a small number of master regulators (transcription
factors, signaling pathway effectors) control large programs of downstream
genes. In the language of the Lyapunov solution @eq-lnm-bio-sigma-integral,
the dominant eigenvectors of $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ correspond to
the most energetic modes of the network---the transcription factor programs
that explain the largest fraction of the compositional variance. The columns
of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ approximate these dominant modes. Clustering on
the rows of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ (each row corresponding to a gene)
therefore groups genes by shared regulatory control, not by artefactual
likelihood coupling.

**The diagonal $\underline{d}$.** The residual diagonal captures the
gene-intrinsic noise: the component of each gene's variance that is not
explained by the shared regulatory programs in $\underline{\underline{W}}$. In
terms of the Lyapunov equation, this is the variance arising from each gene's
own burst noise ($\underline{\underline{D}}$) and degradation ($J_{gg}$), after
subtracting the variance propagated through the network.

The assumption that $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ is approximately low-rank
is therefore not merely a computational convenience for handling
$G = 20{,}000$ genes---it is a biological expectation. The number of
independent transcription-factor programs driving compositional variation within
a cell type is vastly smaller than the number of genes. Typical estimates of the
number of active transcription-factor programs in a mammalian cell type range
from tens to low hundreds, precisely the range of $k$ used in practice.

**Identifiability of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ up to rotation.** A caveat
that affects the biological interpretation of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ is
that the likelihood depends on $\underline{\underline{W}}$ only through the
product $\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top$. For any
$k \times k$ orthogonal matrix $\underline{\underline{R}}$ (i.e.,
$\underline{\underline{R}}\,\underline{\underline{R}}^\top =
\underline{\underline{I}}_k$), the substitution
$\underline{\underline{W}} \to
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{R}}$ leaves
$\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top$ invariant and
produces an identical model. Consequently:

- The **column space** of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ (equivalently, the
  rank-$k$ subspace of log-ratio space spanned by the regulatory programs) is
  identifiable from the data.
- **Specific columns** of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ (e.g., "program 1" vs
  "program 2") are *not* identifiable without an additional constraint such as
  sparsity, orthogonality of gene loadings, or varimax-style rotation.

For downstream uses that are rotation-invariant---clustering genes by the rows
of $\underline{\underline{W}}$, computing gene--gene similarity via
$\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top$, extracting the
rank-$k$ correlation structure---the lack of column-level identification is
harmless. For uses that require *interpretable* programs (e.g., naming columns
of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ as TF activities), an additional rotation or
sparsification step is required as a post-processing operation.

## Connecting the two models: the independent-gene limit {#sec-lnm-bio-connection}

The biophysical derivation clarifies the relationship between the
Dirichlet-Multinomial and the logistic-normal models. They arise from different
approximation regimes applied to the same underlying stochastic gene expression
process.

**The DM regime (exact, independent genes).** When genes are transcriptionally
independent, $b_g(\underline{\lambda}) = b_g$ in @eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode, and the
Jacobian $\underline{\underline{J}}$ is diagonal (each gene's dynamics depend
only on itself). The exact steady-state of the birth-death process for each gene
is Gamma-distributed (@eq-lnm-bio-gamma-recover), and normalizing yields the
Dirichlet. This is the derivation in @sec-dirichlet-multinomial. No linear noise
approximation is involved; the result is exact given the birth-death model.

**The LNM regime (approximate, interacting genes).** When genes interact,
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ has off-diagonal entries, and the exact joint
steady-state distribution of $G$ coupled birth-death processes has no tractable
closed form. The LNA in log-space gives the multivariate Gaussian approximation
@eq-lnm-bio-mvn, from which the logistic-normal on compositions follows.

**The two regimes converge for non-interacting, high-expression genes.** When
$\underline{\underline{J}}$ is diagonal and $r_g$ is large, the Gamma
distribution for $\lambda_g$ is well approximated by a log-normal (by the
central limit theorem applied to the log of a Gamma with large shape
parameter). In this limit, both models produce approximately the same marginal
distributions and the same (negative, simplex-constrained) pairwise
correlations. The DM and the diagonal-$\Sigma$ LNM are not mathematically
identical, but they are asymptotically equivalent in the high-expression regime.

The key difference emerges for **interacting genes**: the Dirichlet model has no
mechanism to represent the cross-gene correlations generated by
$\underline{\underline{J}}$, regardless of how large $r_g$ is. The
logistic-normal does, through the off-diagonal entries of
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$.

**The asymmetry of "extending" DM.** It is tempting to describe the LNM as a
strict generalization of the DM (with the DM recovered as the
diagonal-$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, high-expression limit). The
relationship is not actually symmetric, and it is worth being precise about the
direction of the trade-off:

- For **non-interacting genes**, the DM is the *exact* steady-state of the
  underlying birth-death process, regardless of expression level. The LNM is the
  LNA approximation of the same process and degrades for genes with very low
  counts, where neither the drift linearization nor the constant-noise
  approximation is reliable (see @sec-lnm-bio-lna).
- For **interacting genes**, the DM cannot represent the cross-gene correlations
  generated by off-diagonal entries of $\underline{\underline{J}}$ at all, while
  the LNM does so through $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$.

The LNM therefore trades *exactness for the non-interacting, low-expression
regime* against *expressivity in the coupled regime*. This is the correct
trade-off for our scientific goal (recovering gene-gene structure), but it does
mean that for genes that are both weakly coupled and lowly expressed, the DM
remains the better marginal model. The LNM is a generalization only in the sense
of admitting a broader class of *covariance structures*, not in the sense of
being a uniformly better description of the underlying biophysics.

## Summary of the derivation {#sec-lnm-bio-summary}

The following table summarizes the two models and the biophysical assumptions
from which they arise:

| Model | Biophysical assumption | Steady-state | Correlation structure |
|-------|------------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| DM | Independent promoters ($\underline{\underline{J}}$ diagonal) | Product of Gammas (exact) | Fixed by $\underline{r}$; always negative on simplex |
| LNM | Interacting genes ($\underline{\underline{J}}$ general) | Multivariate Gaussian in log-space (LNA) | Free $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, determined by GRN via Lyapunov |

The derivation proceeded through the following chain of reasoning:

1. **mRNA as a filtered readout** (@sec-lnm-bio-filter): The mRNA count is an
   exponential moving average of the promoter's switching history, with memory
   timescale $1/\gamma_g$ set by mRNA degradation.
2. **The effective Poisson rate** (@sec-lnm-bio-lambda-def): The variable
   $\lambda_g = \hat{r}_g \cdot p_g$ is the expected mRNA abundance, and it
   satisfies an ODE with production and first-order degradation.
3. **Gene regulatory coupling** (@sec-lnm-bio-grn): Regulatory interactions
   enter by making the production term $b_g$ depend on other genes'
   expression levels.
4. **Stochastic dynamics** (@sec-lnm-bio-cle): The CLE adds noise to the
   coupled ODE, modeling intrinsic gene expression stochasticity.
5. **Log-space transformation** (@sec-lnm-bio-log-space): Itô's lemma
   transforms the CLE to log-abundance space.
6. **Linearization** (@sec-lnm-bio-lna): The LNA yields a multivariate OU
   process in log-space.
7. **Steady-state** (@sec-lnm-bio-lyapunov): The OU steady-state is a
   multivariate Gaussian, with covariance given by the Lyapunov equation.
8. **Composition** (@sec-lnm-bio-to-ln): The softmax map from log-abundances to
   compositions yields the logistic-normal.

The logistic-normal model does not abandon the biophysics underlying the
Dirichlet-Multinomial; it adopts a different approximation regime that admits the
cross-gene correlations the DM cannot represent. The covariance matrix
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ that we learn (via its low-rank factorization
$\underline{\underline{W}}\, \underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d})$) is, under the LNA, a direct steady-state
consequence of the cell's underlying gene regulatory network. The price of this
expressivity is the linearization and constant-noise approximations of
@sec-lnm-bio-lna, which limit the model's faithfulness for weakly expressed
genes.

## Universality: the one-state bursty promoter route {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty}

The derivation in the preceding sections started from the **two-state promoter**
model, in which the promoter switches between ON and OFF states and the
stochasticity of $\lambda_g$ arises from the promoter switching dynamics. A
natural question is whether the logistic-normal result depends on this
particular choice of microscopic model, or whether it is more general.

In this section, we argue that the logistic-normal is a **necessary consequence
of two physical facts**---first-order mRNA degradation and coarse-grainable
production---rather than an artifact of a specific promoter model. The argument
is deductive: any microscopic model satisfying these two conditions produces the
same mesoscopic ODE, from which the logistic-normal follows. We then verify
this general claim by working through a concrete alternative microscopic
model---the **one-state bursty promoter**---and showing that it does indeed
converge to the same mesoscopic dynamics.

### The one-state bursty promoter model {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty-model}

Consider a gene $g$ whose promoter is **constitutively active**: there is no
OFF state. Instead of continuous production at a fixed rate, transcription
occurs in discrete **bursts**. Each burst arrives at rate $k_{i,g}$ (the burst
initiation rate) and deposits a random number of mRNA molecules, where the
burst size $B$ follows a geometric distribution with mean $b_g$:

$$
\text{Prob}(B = n) = \frac{1}{1 + b_g} \left(\frac{b_g}{1 + b_g}\right)^n,
\quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$${#eq-lnm-bio-burst-size}

Between bursts, mRNA molecules degrade independently at rate $\gamma_g$, as
before. The key difference from the two-state model is that there is no promoter
switching---the stochasticity comes entirely from the random timing and random
sizes of the bursts.

### The shot-noise representation {#sec-lnm-bio-shot-noise}

Let $\{t_j\}$ be the (random) arrival times of bursts, forming a Poisson
process with rate $k_{i,g}$, and let $B_j$ be the (random) size of the $j$-th
burst. Conditioned on a specific realization of this burst process, the mRNA
count evolves deterministically between bursts (decaying at rate $\gamma_g$)
and jumps upward by $B_j$ at each burst time $t_j$:

$$
\frac{dm_g}{dt} = \sum_j B_j \, \delta(t - t_j) - \gamma_g \, m_g,
$${#eq-lnm-bio-shot-noise-ode}

where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta function. The solution to this ODE is
the **shot-noise process**:

$$
m_g(t) = \sum_{j:\, t_j < t} B_j \, e^{-\gamma_g(t - t_j)}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-shot-noise-solution}

Each burst deposits $B_j$ molecules at time $t_j$, and these molecules decay
exponentially with rate $\gamma_g$. The current mRNA count is the sum of all
surviving contributions from past bursts. This is the one-state analogue of the
exponential moving average we saw in the two-state model---but instead of
averaging over a binary switching signal, we are summing exponentially decaying
contributions from discrete burst events.

### Steady-state distribution: exact Gamma {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty-gamma}

A classical result in the theory of shot-noise processes (and independently
derivable via the Chemical Master Equation and generating functions; see e.g.
Shahrezaei & Swain, *PNAS* 2008) is that the steady-state distribution of the
shot-noise process @eq-lnm-bio-shot-noise-solution with Poisson burst arrivals
and geometric burst sizes is **exactly** a Gamma distribution:

$$
m_g \sim \text{Gamma}\!\left(\frac{k_{i,g}}{\gamma_g},\, b_g\right),
$${#eq-lnm-bio-bursty-gamma}

where $k_{i,g}/\gamma_g$ is the shape parameter (dimensionless burst frequency)
and $b_g$ is the rate parameter (mean burst size). This is a continuous
approximation to the exact discrete NB distribution; the two coincide in the
regime where molecular counts are not too small, which is precisely the regime
where the CLE and LNA are valid.

No approximation beyond the continuous relaxation is involved: the Gamma is the
**exact** steady-state of the one-state bursty promoter. Comparing with the
two-state promoter, where the Gamma arises as the distribution of
$\lambda_g = \hat{r}_g \cdot p_g$ with $p_g \sim \text{Beta}$, we see that
both microscopic models produce Gamma-distributed effective Poisson rates for
independent genes. This is the first hint of universality.

### The effective Poisson rate as a shot-noise variable {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty-lambda}

In the Poisson-Gamma mixture representation of the NB, we identify the
effective Poisson rate $\lambda_g$ with the (continuous) mRNA abundance at
steady state. From the shot-noise solution @eq-lnm-bio-shot-noise-solution,
the dynamics of $\lambda_g$ between and during burst events are:

- **Between bursts**: $\lambda_g$ decays deterministically,
  $$
  \frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} = -\gamma_g \lambda_g.
  $${#eq-lnm-bio-bursty-decay}

- **At a burst event** at time $t_j$: $\lambda_g$ jumps upward by $B_j$,
  $$
  \lambda_g(t_j^+) = \lambda_g(t_j^-) + B_j.
  $${#eq-lnm-bio-bursty-jump}

This defines a **piecewise-deterministic Markov process** (PDMP), just as in the
two-state promoter case, but with a different structure:

| Feature | Two-state PDMP | One-state bursty PDMP |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| Driving signal | Binary indicator $\mathbb{1}_g(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ | Compound Poisson burst train |
| Drift (ON / between bursts) | $\dot{p}_g = \gamma_g(1 - p_g)$ | $\dot{\lambda}_g = -\gamma_g \lambda_g$ |
| Drift (OFF / N/A) | $\dot{p}_g = -\gamma_g p_g$ | N/A (always "ON") |
| Jumps | Continuous relaxation, no jumps in $\lambda$ | Additive jumps $+B_j$ at burst arrivals |
| Steady-state | $p_g \sim \text{Beta}$, hence $\lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}$ | $\lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}$ directly |

Despite the very different microscopic mechanisms, both PDMPs produce a
Gamma-distributed $\lambda_g$ at steady state.

### Coarse-graining the burst process {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty-coarse}

To connect the one-state bursty promoter to the GRN framework, we need to
replace the discrete burst process with a continuous approximation---the
analogue of replacing the binary indicator $\mathbb{1}_g$ with the continuous
function $h_g(\underline{\lambda})$ in the two-state derivation.

The mean production rate from the burst process is the product of the burst
arrival rate and the mean burst size:

$$
\langle \text{production rate} \rangle = k_{i,g} \cdot b_g.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-mean-production}

When the burst rate $k_{i,g}$ is sufficiently high relative to the mRNA
degradation rate $\gamma_g$ (i.e., many bursts arrive within one mRNA
lifetime), the discrete burst train is well approximated by its mean---a
continuous, deterministic production flow. This is the **fluid limit** of the
compound Poisson process: the sum of many small, frequent bursts looks like a
continuous stream.

In this limit, the ODE for $\lambda_g$ becomes

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} = k_{i,g} \cdot b_g - \gamma_g \lambda_g.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-bursty-ode-independent}

This is the one-state bursty promoter analogue of the two-state result
$d\lambda_g/dt = r_g \, \mathbb{1}_g - \gamma_g \lambda_g$
(@eq-lnm-bio-lambda-ode-boxed), with the role of the maximal production rate
$r_g$ times the switching indicator $\mathbb{1}_g$ replaced by the mean
burst production rate $k_{i,g} \cdot b_g$.

### Gene regulatory coupling in the bursty model {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty-grn}

In a gene regulatory network, the burst initiation rate $k_{i,g}$ depends on
the concentrations of the transcription factors that regulate gene $g$. If
transcription factor $g'$ activates gene $g$, then when $\lambda_{g'}$ is high
(more TF protein is available), gene $g$ initiates bursts more frequently. We
therefore write

$$
k_{i,g} \longrightarrow k_{i,g}(\underline{\lambda}),
$${#eq-lnm-bio-bursty-coupling}

making the burst rate a function of the entire expression vector. This is the
bursty-promoter analogue of the two-state model's coarse-graining
$\mathbb{1}_g \to h_g(\underline{\lambda})$.

Substituting into @eq-lnm-bio-bursty-ode-independent, we obtain the coupled
ODE:

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
\underbrace{k_{i,g}(\underline{\lambda}) \cdot b_g}_{b_g(\underline{\lambda})}
- \gamma_g \lambda_g,
\quad g = 1, \ldots, G.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-bursty-grn-ode}

This is **identical in form** to the GRN ODE derived from the two-state
promoter (@eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode):

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
b_g(\underline{\lambda}) - \gamma_g \lambda_g.
$$

The only difference is the physical interpretation of the production term
$b_g(\underline{\lambda})$:

| Route | $b_g(\underline{\lambda})$ | Physical meaning |
|-------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| Two-state | $r_g \cdot h_g(\underline{\lambda})$ | Maximal rate $\times$ GRN-modulated effective ON-fraction |
| One-state bursty | $k_{i,g}(\underline{\lambda}) \cdot b_g$ | GRN-modulated burst rate $\times$ mean burst size |

The degradation term $\gamma_g \lambda_g$ has **exactly the same origin and
meaning** in both cases: it is first-order mRNA degradation. The mRNA
degradation rate $\gamma_g$ sets the memory timescale of the effective Poisson
rate $\lambda_g$, regardless of whether the production mechanism is promoter
switching or discrete bursting.

### The assumptions for the bursty route {#sec-lnm-bio-bursty-assumptions}

The coarse-graining from the discrete burst process to the continuous ODE
@eq-lnm-bio-bursty-grn-ode requires a set of assumptions analogous to those
listed in @sec-lnm-bio-assumptions for the two-state route:

1. **Fluid limit (many bursts per mRNA lifetime).** The burst arrival rate
   $k_{i,g}$ must be high relative to $\gamma_g$, so that the discrete burst
   train is well approximated by its mean production rate. This is the
   bursty-model analogue of the "promoter coarse-graining" assumption in the
   two-state case. For genes with very low burst rates (rare, large bursts),
   the continuous approximation breaks down and the discrete shot-noise
   description is needed.

2. **Protein tracks mRNA.** As in the two-state route, the regulatory effect of
   gene $g'$ on gene $g$ is mediated through protein, but protein abundance is
   proportional to mRNA: $\text{[protein]}_{g'} \propto \lambda_{g'}$. This
   allows writing the burst rate as $k_{i,g}(\underline{\lambda})$.

3. **Constant burst size.** The mean burst size $b_g$ is treated as a
   gene-specific constant, not modulated by the GRN. This is a simplification:
   in principle, burst size could also depend on TF concentrations. Relaxing
   this assumption would allow $b_g$ to depend on $\underline{\lambda}$, which
   simply redefines $b_g(\underline{\lambda})$ without changing the form of the
   ODE.

4. **First-order mRNA degradation.** Identical to the two-state assumption.

### Convergence of both routes {#sec-lnm-bio-convergence}

From @eq-lnm-bio-bursty-grn-ode onward, the derivation is **identical** to the
two-state route. The GRN ODE has the same form, so the same chain of
approximations applies:

1. The Chemical Langevin Equation adds stochastic noise to the ODE
   (@sec-lnm-bio-cle). The noise amplitude differs slightly between the two
   microscopic models:
   - **Two-state**: $\sigma_g^2(\lambda_g) \propto \lambda_g$ (Poisson-like
     scaling from the telegraph process).
   - **One-state bursty**: $\sigma_g^2(\lambda_g) \propto \lambda_g (1 + b_g)$
     (the factor $(1 + b_g)$ is the Fano factor correction from burst-size
     variability; when bursts produce more than one molecule on average, the
     variance is super-Poissonian).

2. Itô's lemma transforms to log-space (@sec-lnm-bio-log-space).

3. The LNA linearizes the drift and freezes the noise at its steady-state
   value (@sec-lnm-bio-lna), yielding a multivariate OU process.

4. The Lyapunov equation gives the steady-state covariance
   (@sec-lnm-bio-lyapunov).

5. The softmax map yields the logistic-normal on compositions
   (@sec-lnm-bio-to-ln).

The only place the microscopic model enters the final result is in the
**diffusion matrix** $\underline{\underline{D}}$. For the two-state model,
$D_{gg} = 1/(2\bar{\lambda}_g)$; for the one-state bursty model,
$D_{gg} = (1 + b_g)/(2\bar{\lambda}_g)$. This affects the *diagonal* entries
of the steady-state covariance $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ (each gene's
marginal variance in log-space), but **not the qualitative structure**: the
covariance is still determined by the Lyapunov equation, the off-diagonal
entries are still generated by the GRN Jacobian
$\underline{\underline{J}}$, and the logistic-normal form is unchanged.

### Why the convergence is not a coincidence {#sec-lnm-bio-universality}

The fact that two very different microscopic models produce the same mesoscopic
ODE is not an empirical coincidence discovered by checking models one at a time.
It is a **necessary consequence of two physical ingredients** that any model of
mRNA dynamics must satisfy:

1. **First-order mRNA degradation.** Each mRNA molecule is independently
   degraded at rate $\gamma_g$. This is unimolecular chemistry: the
   degradation flux from a pool of expected abundance $\lambda_g$ is always
   $\gamma_g \lambda_g$, regardless of how those molecules were produced. No
   model of transcription changes this term.

2. **Coarse-grainable production.** Whatever the microscopic production
   mechanism---promoter switching, discrete bursting, polymerase kinetics,
   chromatin remodeling, or any combination thereof---once the fast production
   dynamics are averaged out (under an appropriate timescale separation), the
   effective production rate is *some* function $b_g(\underline{\lambda})$ of
   the current regulatory state.

Given these two ingredients, conservation of mass dictates the ODE:

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
\underbrace{b_g(\underline{\lambda})}_{\text{production (any mechanism)}}
- \underbrace{\gamma_g \lambda_g}_{\text{first-order degradation}}.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-universal-ode}

This is not a model-specific result; it is the unique ODE consistent with
linear degradation and an arbitrary production function. The two-state and
one-state bursty derivations above are **concrete verifications** that the
coarse-graining step (ingredient 2) succeeds for specific, well-studied
microscopic models. But the ODE form itself is guaranteed by ingredient 1 alone.

The downstream chain of approximations (CLE $\to$ log-space $\to$ LNA $\to$ OU
$\to$ Lyapunov $\to$ logistic-normal) depends only on this mesoscopic ODE, not
on the microscopic details that produced it. The microscopic model affects only
the noise amplitude $\sigma_g$, which determines the diagonal of the diffusion
matrix $\underline{\underline{D}}$---a quantitative detail that influences each
gene's marginal variance but not the qualitative structure of the result.

We may therefore state the result precisely:

> **The logistic-normal distribution on gene compositions is the steady-state
> distribution of any gene regulatory network whose effective dynamics satisfy
> (i) first-order mRNA degradation and (ii) a production rate that, after
> coarse-graining the fast transcription kinetics, depends on the expression
> levels of other genes. Under the linear noise approximation in log-abundance
> space, these two conditions are sufficient to produce a multivariate Gaussian
> on log-abundances and hence a logistic-normal on compositions. The
> microscopic promoter model enters only through the noise structure, not
> through the form of the result.**

This strengthens the biophysical justification for the logistic-normal: the
result does not depend on committing to a particular model of promoter kinetics.
The two-state promoter and the one-state bursty promoter are the two most widely
studied microscopic models in the gene expression literature, and both converge
to the same mesoscopic ODE. Any other microscopic model satisfying first-order
degradation and coarse-grainable production will do the same.
